one_line_takeaway: PromptGuard appends a per-category-trained soft 'system prompt' embedding to Stable Diffusion's text encoder, cutting NSFW Unsafe Ratio to 5.84% (vs. 39.21% undefended, 12.54% best baseline) and holding up under SneakyPrompt/MMA-Diffusion adversarial attacks (2.35% average vs. 60.47% undefended), at roughly the same inference cost as unmodified SD (1.39s vs. 1.38s/image).
key_finding: On the Multi-headed Classifier, PromptGuard achieves the lowest average Unsafe Ratio of 5.84% across four malicious categories (Sexually Explicit 1.50%, Violent 5.17%, Political 12.17%, Disturbing 4.50%), versus 39.21% for undefended SDv1.4 and the next-best baseline UCE at 12.54%. Under LlavaGuard, PromptGuard averages 6.18% versus 38.46% (SDv1.4) and 14.00% (UCE, closest baseline). CLIP Score for benign preservation is 25.96 (vs. 26.52 undefended), with the drop kept under 0.5 versus drops exceeding 1.0 for UCE, SLD, and POSI; LPIPS is roughly on par with baselines (0.646 vs. range 0.625-0.655). Efficiency: AvgTime is 1.39 s/image, essentially matching undefended SDv1.4 (1.38 s/image) and 3.8x faster than the slowest defenses (SLD-Max at 7.06 s/image, UCE at 6.03 s/image, POSI at 6.15 s/image), since PromptGuard adds no extra model or diffusion-process modification. Under adversarial attacks (Multi-headed Classifier), PromptGuard's average Unsafe Ratio is 2.35% (MMA-Diffusion 5.53%, SneakyPrompt-N 0.00%, SneakyPrompt-P 1.51%) versus SDv1.4's 60.47% and the next-best baseline UCE's 6.03%; under LlavaGuard it is 3.69% vs. SDv1.4's 63.32%. A scalability experiment adding a new 'self-harm' category shows the integrated embedding (original + self-harm) achieves the lowest Unsafe Ratio (10.33%) versus SDv1.4 (44.50%), original-only PromptGuard (14.50%), and self-harm-only PromptGuard (23.50%), without materially degrading CLIP/LPIPS. Ablations on the weighting parameter lambda show optimal moderation typically at lambda 0.6-0.7 per category, and optimization-step sweeps show diminishing but continued Unsafe Ratio reduction up to 3000 steps (2.5% for sexually explicit content) with a 1000-1500 step 'practical' sweet spot (~6.5%).
core_contribution: PromptGuard introduces a universal 'safety soft prompt' P* for text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models, drawing an analogy to the system-prompt mechanism used to align LLMs. Because T2I models have no interface for injecting system-level instructions, the authors add a new trainable token (vocabulary-expanded embedding row) to the text encoder and optimize it directly in the continuous token-embedding space rather than searching discrete text. Training uses a divide-and-conquer strategy: a separate soft-prompt embedding is optimized per NSFW category (sexually explicit, violent, political, disturbing), and at inference time the trained embeddings are concatenated and appended to every user prompt as an implicit system prompt. Two losses are used per sample: Lb (benign preservation, matching the standard SD denoising objective on benign prompt/image pairs) and Lm (malicious moderation via contrastive-style guidance, pushing predicted noise away from the original unsafe image's noise and toward a SDEdit-generated 'safer' counterpart of the same image), balanced by hyperparameter lambda. The method requires no external filter/classifier model and does not modify the base T2I model's parameters, only the appended embedding.
threat_model: Defender is a T2I service operator seeking to suppress NSFW output (sexual, violent, political, disturbing content) generated from both natural-language malicious prompts and adversarially crafted prompts. Attackers are modeled via two existing red-teaming frameworks evaluated against the defense: SneakyPrompt (RL-optimized adversarial prompts using natural-word and pseudo-word variants, designed to evade closed-box safety filters like DALL-E 2's) and MMA-Diffusion (multimodal adversarial attack combining text prompt manipulation with image perturbations to bypass both text filters and post-hoc image safety checkers). The paper does not propose new attacks; it evaluates PromptGuard's robustness as a defense against these pre-existing attack methods using their public datasets (SneakyPrompt-N/P, MMA-Diffusion Nudity dataset with pseudo words).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) no large-scale human evaluation, since exposure of annotators to toxic content was avoided for ethical reasons, so the reported metrics lack human-perception nuance especially for borderline/aesthetic cases; (2) dependence on automated proxy evaluators (Multi-head Classifier and LlavaGuard) means reported safety numbers are bounded by these classifiers' own detection blind spots, which could propagate into the reported results despite using two evaluators to reduce single-classifier bias. Additional caveats visible in the paper's own text: the taxonomy is coarse-grained (four umbrella categories), with subcategories like self-harm and hate speech folded implicitly into Violent/Disturbing/Political rather than handled as first-class categories (though the paper demonstrates modular extension to self-harm as a mitigation); the SafeGen baseline had to be re-trained on the authors' own dataset because public SafeGen weights were sexual-content-only, which is a fairness adjustment but also means that baseline's published numbers elsewhere may differ; the paper explicitly deprioritizes cross-model transferability in favor of within-model defense strength, and full transferability results are deferred to an extended (non-reviewed-here) version of the paper.

datasets_used: MMA-Diffusion Nudity dataset with pseudo words (adversarial)
789 curated malicious training prompts (Unsafe Diffusion dataset 27.25%, NSFW data scraper 25.35%, GPT-4o mini synthetic 47.40%)
200 filtered synthetic benign training prompts (from ~500 GPT-4o candidates across animals, food, people, landscapes, transportation, home scenes)
quantitative_results: Average Unsafe Ratio (Multi-headed Classifier, natural-language malicious prompts): PromptGuard 5.84% vs. SDv1.4 39.21% vs. best baseline UCE 12.54%
Average Unsafe Ratio (LlavaGuard, natural-language malicious prompts): PromptGuard 6.18% vs. SDv1.4 38.46% vs. best baseline UCE 14.00%
CLIP Score (benign preservation): PromptGuard 25.96 vs. undefended SDv1.4 26.52 (drop <0.5, vs. >1.0 for UCE/SLD/POSI)
LPIPS: PromptGuard 0.646 vs. SDv1.4 0.637 (comparable to other baselines, range 0.625-0.655)
AvgTime: PromptGuard 1.39 s/image vs. SDv1.4 1.38 s/image; PromptGuard is 3.8x faster than slowest baselines (SLD-Max 7.06s, POSI 6.15s, UCE 6.03s)
Average adversarial Unsafe Ratio (Multi-headed Classifier, SneakyPrompt-N/P + MMA-Diffusion): PromptGuard 2.35% vs. SDv1.4 60.47% vs. best baseline UCE 6.03%
Average adversarial Unsafe Ratio (LlavaGuard): PromptGuard 3.69% vs. SDv1.4 63.32% vs. best baseline UCE 12.73%
SneakyPrompt-N Unsafe Ratio (Multi-headed Classifier): PromptGuard 0.00% vs. SDv1.4 52.26%
Self-harm scalability test Unsafe Ratio: Integrated PromptGuard (original+self-harm) 10.33% vs. SDv1.4 44.50% vs. original-only 14.50% vs. self-harm-only 23.50%

Optimal lambda per category (Unsafe Ratio minimum at 1000 steps): Sexually Explicit 3.50% at lambda=0.7; Violent 13.50% at lambda=0.6; Political 6.00-7.00% at lambda=0.4/0.7; Disturbing 3.00% at lambda=0.7
Optimization-step sweep (sexually explicit): Unsafe Ratio 22.50% (500 steps) down to 2.50% (3000 steps), with ~6.5% around 1000-1500 steps
baselines_compared: UCE (model alignment: erases concepts via text projection matrices)
SafeGen (model alignment: self-attention optimization; re-trained on authors' dataset for fair comparison)
SafetyFilter (SD v1.4 default output-stage NSFW image filter)
POSI (LLM-based prompt rewriting to safe alternatives)
